jokes.text: The cold open plays extended moaning and sex sounds over what turns out to be a porn video — the punchline is the man saying 'yeah, that's a nice beach ball' mid-moan, revealing this is scripted/absurd porn dialogue
jokes.setup: Sounds of intense sexual moaning suggest a sex scene is happening onscreen; the audience leans into the apparent pornographic content
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, that's a nice beach ball...' — completely mundane, almost sweet compliment dropped into pornographic moaning, undercutting the heat with absurd banality
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant cold open: the sustained moan-then-mundane-compliment is a perfectly timed deflation. 'Beach ball' is chosen for maximum bathos — it's not just neutral, it's childlike and innocent. Strong misdirection that pays off the episode's central premise (Trey is a porn star) while being genuinely funny on its own. Rewatch bonus because knowing the full context makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Abbi eats cold fries from the bathroom floor — 'Good, 'cause they're from last night. Someone left them on the floor in the bathroom.'
jokes.setup: Man asks if she's eating those fries, seemingly concerned she'd eat fries that were 'his'
jokes.punchline: They're from last night, found on the bathroom floor — the concern was about them being stale and gross, not his
jokes.explanation: Efficient and character-specific — Abbi's broke-girl desperation is established immediately. The bathroom floor detail escalates the grossness past where you expected it to land. The beat of her continuing to eat them after learning this information is the real joke.

jokes.text: Job interviewer says a 'fucking idiot' could put wristbands on people; Abbi responds 'Oh, I'm a... I'm a huge fucking idiot' as a sincere pitch for her own qualifications
jokes.setup: The interviewer tries to lowball the job requirements as an insult; it's meant to be discouraging
jokes.punchline: Abbi takes it as a genuine compliment and qualification, fully earnest — 'I'm a huge fucking idiot'
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent joke. The structure is tight: the interviewer uses 'idiot' as a pejorative barrier, and Abbi's complete sincerity in claiming the label inverts the power dynamic while deepening her character. It's quintessentially Abbi — self-deprecating without self-pity, earnest without irony, specific to her desperate optimism. The economy is nearly perfect; the pivot from insult to credential is instantaneous. Comfortably earns the 8+ score.

jokes.text: 'You're not wristband material' — the formal pomposity of the rejection for a task explicitly described as one a 'fucking idiot' could do
jokes.setup: Job was already framed as requiring zero competence
jokes.punchline: Rejection is delivered with the gravitas of a rejected PhD candidate
jokes.explanation: Solid institutional absurdism — the gap between the job's triviality and the formal language of rejection is funny and recognizable. Doesn't reach beyond competent but it works.

jokes.text: Abbi threatens 'You're gonna rue the day' after being rejected for a wristband job
jokes.setup: Abbi was rejected from a job she defined herself as perfectly unqualified for
jokes.punchline: She exits with theatrical dignity, issuing a grandiose threat completely disproportionate to the stakes
jokes.explanation: A reliable character beat — Abbi's dignity in defeat. 'Rue the day' is archaic and theatrical, which is the joke. The interviewer's bored 'I'm sure' is the perfect flat cap.

jokes.text: 'I'm just going to be digging out other people's pubes till I'm a great-grandmother'
jokes.setup: Abbi is lamenting her career prospects after another rejection
jokes.punchline: Her vision of her worst-case future is hyper-specific — not 'stuck at this job' but the surreal and disgusting detail of decades of pube-digging
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'great-grandmother' and the image of an elderly Abbi still picking out gym pubes is both funny and genuinely melancholy. The combination of grotesque detail with existential despair is signature Broad City. Strong character integration — this is Abbi's particular flavor of self-pity.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'Amber alert. Urgency.' as her phone sign-off, then hangs up without giving Abbi any of the details she needs
jokes.setup: Ilana calls with an urgent job opportunity and uses 'Amber alert' to signal urgency
jokes.punchline: She hangs up immediately without providing where or when, leaving Abbi saying 'What coffee shop, what time, what? Never any details.'
jokes.explanation: Efficient character-defining comedy. Ilana's chaotic self-absorption expressed through the gap between her dramatic urgency and complete uselessness is a reliable engine for the show. 'Never any details' is Abbi's perfectly exasperated reaction beat.

jokes.text: Oliver's mom gives Abbi-level detailed instructions: 'no soy', then recommends Babbo, Nobu, or Momofuku as fallback options for a kid's lunch
jokes.setup: Mom is giving Ilana sober, caring dietary restrictions for her child
jokes.punchline: The 'safe' backup restaurants are all upscale NYC establishments costing $40+ per dish — completely oblivious to the absurdity from a childcare perspective
jokes.explanation: A sharp class-comedy joke. The specificity of three actual prestigious NYC restaurants makes it land harder than generic 'fancy place' references. Sets up the entire class-gap dynamic of the subplot efficiently.

jokes.text: Ilana makes a phone call mid-conversation with her employer; when called out, she responds 'Did I?' with complete sincerity
jokes.setup: Ilana is in a professional conversation with a wealthy employer
jokes.punchline: She interrupts it to call Abbi without acknowledging the social violation, then gaslights herself about whether it happened
jokes.explanation: 'Did I?' is a beautifully casual bit of Ilana self-absorption. The joke is that she's not performing innocence — she genuinely may not have registered that she did something rude. Strong character integration, efficiently executed.

jokes.text: Ilana responds to instructions about Oliver's dietary restrictions and where to take him ('Babbo or Nobu or Momofuku') with 'YOLO, FOMO, tippy-tee'
jokes.setup: Employer rattles off upscale restaurant names as if they're obvious options for a child's Saturday lunch
jokes.punchline: Ilana responds with a string of internet slang and a nonsense syllable as if it's an equivalent acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: 'Tippy-tee' is the piece that elevates this from a simple YOLO joke into something absurdist. The rhythm of the three-part response mirrors the three-restaurant list perfectly, making it a structural parody of the employer's phrasing. Distinctly Ilana's brand of chaos disguised as competence.

jokes.text: Employer announces she's going to 'interview Desi Tutu and wax with Martha Plimpton' as her Saturday plans — delivered as completely normal
jokes.setup: Rich woman giving Ilana Saturday instructions
jokes.punchline: Her Saturday itinerary involves celebrity name-drops delivered as mundane errands — 'interview Desi Tutu' is especially strange and unexplained
jokes.explanation: The deadpan absurdity of 'interview Desi Tutu' (a seemingly invented celebrity name) is a decent background gag about wealthy social worlds. Not deeply constructed but adds texture.

jokes.text: Ilana calls the employer 'what a bad bitch' immediately after she leaves
jokes.setup: Rich employer just gave instructions and name-dropped celebrities
jokes.punchline: Ilana's genuine admiration expressed through her own slang framework — the compliment is sincere but the language is jarring in context
jokes.explanation: Quick, character-consistent beat. Ilana's admiration for confident women (regardless of class) is a consistent character note. Not a standout but efficient.

jokes.text: Ilana greets Oliver: 'Last time I saw you, you were a toothless spud sitting in your own poop'
jokes.setup: Ilana reunites with Oliver at the coffee shop as if he's an old acquaintance
jokes.punchline: She greets a young child the way you'd greet a college friend after a rough year — 'toothless spud sitting in your own poop' is grotesquely vivid but delivered with warmth
jokes.explanation: 'Toothless spud sitting in your own poop' is specific, evocative, and delivered with real affection — the joke is that Ilana treats this child like a peer, and the image she conjures is both accurate (babies do this) and hilarious. Strong economy and character-specific warmth.

jokes.text: Ilana's four R's of environmentalism: 'Reduce, re-use, recycle, Rihanna!'
jokes.setup: Ilana lectures Oliver on environmentalism using the classic three Rs
jokes.punchline: She extends to four — the fourth being Rihanna, presented with equal conviction to the other three
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes. The rhythm is perfect — the three real Rs create expectation, and 'Rihanna' lands as both absurd and completely, sincerely Ilana. It's not a non-sequitur; it's a statement of values. Ilana treats Rihanna as a genuine pillar of her worldview equal to recycling. The economy is flawless: five words after setup. Highly quotable, distinctly character-specific, impossible to imagine any other character on TV saying this. Justifiably earns 8+.

jokes.text: Barista's flat refusal: 'I'm sorry ma'am, we can't reuse cups. It's a health code violation.' — followed by Ilana's 'Come on. This is so gross.'
jokes.setup: Ilana tries to get a refill in a used cup as part of her environmental pitch
jokes.punchline: The barista shuts it down with mundane institutional logic; Ilana's 'this is so gross' is ambiguous — is she disgusted by waste or by the health code? The reversal makes the barista the buzzkill and Ilana the reasonable one
jokes.explanation: Functional but not exceptional. The ambiguity of 'this is so gross' is the best element — it could mean the waste is gross or the cup is gross. Nice small beat following the Rihanna joke.

jokes.text: Ilana on Oliver's future: 'If I didn't step in, he could go to Yale. And then Yale law and then kill a stripper and settle out of court.'
jokes.setup: Ilana explains her mission to 'mold' Oliver and protect him from a bad future
jokes.punchline: The 'bad future' she's protecting him from is the Yale-to-stripper-murder pipeline — presented as a logical and inevitable consequence of elite education
jokes.explanation: Exceptional construction. The joke escalates Yale → Yale law → kill a stripper → settle out of court as if each is the inevitable next step, delivered with absolute earnestness. It's simultaneously a class critique, a satire of elite male impunity, and completely absurd. 'Settle out of court' is the perfect bureaucratic cap that makes it funnier than just 'kill a stripper' alone. The specificity (Yale law, not just law school) and the settlement detail show genuine comedic craft. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Ilana's rationale: 'If I don't do something, he could turn into another useless rich old white man'
jokes.setup: Following the Yale/stripper joke, Ilana summarizes her mission
jokes.punchline: The summation of her greatest fear for Oliver — not poverty, not crime, but becoming 'a useless rich old white man' — delivered as genuine existential dread
jokes.explanation: Good callback that lands the political subtext, but less funny than the Yale joke because it makes the point explicit. Still works as a beat.

jokes.text: Ilana watching porn in a coffee shop with a child present — Abbi's horrified 'Ilana, I'm not watching porn in here'
jokes.setup: The 'job opportunity' Ilana promised turns out to be watching porn in a public coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Abbi's protest and Ilana's immediate, completely unbothered correction: 'First of all, everybody's watching porn in here.'
jokes.explanation: 'First of all, everybody's watching porn in here' is a great Ilana line — it normalizes the socially transgressive with casual confidence. The 'first of all' implies there are additional points to come, which is its own little joke structure.

jokes.text: Ilana's porn taxonomy: 'I don't watch anything but solo porn because regular porn is like, shut up little girl, wash my feet!' — her full description of mainstream porn's dialogue
jokes.setup: Ilana explains her feminist porn consumption habits
jokes.punchline: Her characterization of mainstream porn dialogue — 'shut up little girl, wash my feet!' — is a deliberately absurd/gross parody that conveys genuine critique through comedy
jokes.explanation: The foot-washing detail is strange and specific enough to be genuinely funny — it suggests mainstream porn has bizarre demands even beyond the sexual. Ilana's critical take delivered through committed parody is both funny and substantive.

jokes.text: Ilana's extended parody of mainstream porn's 'barely legal' dialogue, including 'I love shaved pubes and tan, crispy bellies and tits'
jokes.setup: Ilana continues her critique of mainstream porn by performing its dialogue
jokes.punchline: 'Crispy bellies' — the specific, strange, sensory word choice among otherwise expected porn-dialogue clichés makes the whole parody suddenly surreal
jokes.explanation: 'Crispy bellies' is the standout detail — it's both disgusting and specific in a way that suggests Ilana has very strong opinions about body presentation in porn. The extended monologue is slightly overlong but the individual details are funny.

jokes.text: Ilana's porn search path: 'So I started with average-size penis jerk. That led me to twins squirting, hello. Which then brought me to frosted tips masturbation.'
jokes.setup: Ilana describes her search journey to finding Trey's porn
jokes.punchline: 'Frosted tips masturbation' — the specific combination of a very early-2000s hair trend with a porn category is completely unexpected and specific in a way that's genuinely strange and funny. The logical progression of the search history creates a comedic journey.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Average-size penis jerk → twins squirting → frosted tips masturbation' is a perfect comedic escalation where each step is stranger than the last. 'Hello' after 'twins squirting' is Ilana's perfectly timed editorial comment. 'Frosted tips masturbation' achieves comedy through hyper-specific anachronism — it evokes a precise cultural moment (late 90s/early 2000s boy band aesthetic) and applies it to pornography in a way that is both absurd and somehow completely logical for Ilana's taste. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Whoa, I just usually Google "porn".' — as a confessional reaction to Ilana's elaborate search taxonomy
jokes.setup: Ilana has just described an elaborate, curated, feminist porn discovery journey
jokes.punchline: Abbi's approach is just 'porn' — no curation, no preferences, no ideology. The contrast between Ilana's specificity and Abbi's blankness is the whole joke.
jokes.explanation: Possibly the episode's most economical joke. Four words ('I just Google porn') after Ilana's elaborate monologue creates perfect comedic contrast. It's pure character definition — Ilana is specific, ideological, and exploratory; Abbi is blunt, practical, and uncurated. Maximum funny per word, specific to Abbi's character, earned entirely by the setup. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Ilana's 'That's probably why I really, really don't like most of it' — followed by her suggesting Abbi should check her search history
jokes.setup: Abbi admits to just Googling 'porn'
jokes.punchline: Ilana diagnoses Abbi's dissatisfaction with porn as a direct consequence of her search laziness, dispensing feminist porn-navigation advice as sincerely as a therapist
jokes.explanation: Nice extension of the bit — Ilana as porn guidance counselor is character-consistent. The joke is in her sincere helpfulness. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: Discovery reveal: 'Oh my god, Trey is in a porn! Kirk Steele? Hold on—' — the reveal that Abbi's boss is a porn star, followed by Abbi's panicked repetition
jokes.setup: The entire cold open and the porn taxonomy scene have been building to this moment — the porn they found starred Trey
jokes.punchline: The episode title is a porn name. 'So before Trey was my boss, he was a porn star? So, so I'm taking orders from a porn star.'
jokes.explanation: A well-structured act break joke. The cold open recontextualizes beautifully — 'that's a nice beach ball' becomes funnier in retrospect. Abbi's repetition of 'taking orders from a porn star' in disbelief is good character comedy. Strong structural payoff.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'So before Trey was my boss, he was a porn star? So I'm taking orders from a porn star.'
jokes.setup: Abbi processes the revelation that her gym boss had a porn career
jokes.punchline: The inversion — not 'he was a porn star who became my boss' but 'I'm taking orders from a porn star' — the present tense reframe makes it funnier, framing Trey's authority as now absurd
jokes.explanation: Functional processing-the-reveal beat. The present tense 'taking orders from a porn star' is the right comedic choice — it's about power dynamics, not just biography.

jokes.text: Ilana's advice: 'Use it, bitch.'
jokes.setup: Abbi spirals about what to do with the information that her boss is a former porn star
jokes.punchline: Ilana cuts through all moral complexity with three words of blackmail advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Ilana's entire moral philosophy and practical advice distilled to three words. The gap between Abbi's anxious spiral and Ilana's instant, cheerful pragmatism is the joke. Highly quotable and distinctly character-specific.

jokes.text: Trey adopts a brief British accent to announce the 'bloody fucking spill' then immediately drops it: 'But really, there's a bloody fucking spill in the spin studio'
jokes.setup: Trey delivers gym-management news to Abbi
jokes.punchline: He opens with a full British accent delivery, then immediately translates it into plain speech as if the accent were an optional flourish he's choosing to abandon
jokes.explanation: The British accent bit is a nice character quirk for Trey — it suggests he had affectations from his porn days that occasionally leak out. The 'but really' translation implies even he knows it was unnecessary. Solid and specific.

jokes.text: Abbi fakes illness to avoid cleaning: 'I gotta relax before I teach the 12:30 kick ass kettle bell class' — she has no such class; Trey's confused 'What are you talking about right now?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to leverage the Kirk Steele secret for better work assignments
jokes.punchline: Her improvised excuse is both a lie and a completely false claim about her own job responsibilities — she's inventing a class she doesn't teach
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the blackmail plot. The fake phlegm cough is a nice visual beat. Trey's genuine bewilderment is well-played.

jokes.text: Abbi performs an extended, disgusting throat-clearing as part of her fake illness: 'Whoa, that is a lot of phlegm, isn't it?'
jokes.setup: Abbi claims to be coming down with something
jokes.punchline: She produces a comically extreme amount of phlegm-clearing, then comments on it herself with genuine surprise — even she's impressed
jokes.explanation: The self-commentary 'that is a lot of phlegm, isn't it' redeems what would otherwise be a basic gross-out gag. Abbi's own bewilderment at her performance adds a layer.

jokes.text: Trey: 'Abbi, that trick is for after hours, okay?' followed by Abbi's quietly devastating '...Kirk?'
jokes.setup: Trey dismisses Abbi's fake illness, saying it's an 'after hours' thing — implying the fake-sick move is a known tactic he tolerates only at certain times
jokes.punchline: Abbi's single-word response — just his porn name, nothing else — is her entire negotiating position delivered with perfect minimalism
jokes.explanation: The single best-structured joke in the episode. The entire blackmail premise is compressed into one word. Abbi doesn't explain, threaten, or negotiate — she just says his porn name quietly and lets the weight of it land. The comedy comes from the enormous power contained in that one word, and Abbi's dawning confidence in wielding it. Perfect economy (one word after setup), perfectly timed, and the rewatch value is enormous because knowing the full Trey/Kirk mythology makes it land harder. Earns 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: Trey's office is revealed to be a storage closet containing Abbi's personal items — he insists 'it's your office, all your stuff's in here'
jokes.setup: Trey says 'let's go to your office right now'; Abbi points out she doesn't have an office
jokes.punchline: The 'office' is clearly not an office, but contains her snake, cardigan, tweezers, and a childhood pug photo — evidence of her gym-worker life that Trey frames as officeness
jokes.explanation: The list of Abbi's personal items is funny in its specificity — particularly 'your snake' (a real snake? a snake figurine?) and 'your childhood pug' photo. The gap between 'office' and 'storage closet with someone's sad belongings' is a good visual gag about Abbi's status in the gym.

jokes.text: Abbi's defeated capitulation: 'Fine, it's my fucking office.'
jokes.setup: Trey has listed her personal items as proof it's her office
jokes.punchline: Abbi accepts the framing completely — once he enumerates her belongings, the logic is unassailable and she surrenders
jokes.explanation: Good beat. Abbi accepting her low status under Trey's framing is consistent and funny. The exhausted 'fine' suggests she's run out of will to fight the definition.

jokes.text: Trey offers to go down on Abbi in exchange for her silence: 'What do you want, money?' / 'No.' / 'I'll go down on you right now.' / 'No, what?'
jokes.setup: Trey asks what Abbi wants in exchange for keeping his secret; she refuses money
jokes.punchline: His second offer is oral sex — delivered as if it's the most reasonable escalation from cash; 'No, what?' is Abbi's perfect baffled rejection of the entire premise
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the sequence: money declined → oral sex offered as alternative. Trey's logic implies these are equivalent forms of compensation, which both characterizes him and is absurd. His continued insistence ('I think it would be in your best interest') is the running escalation.

jokes.text: Abbi's actual ask: 'I want to teach the 12:30 kick ass kettle bell class.' Trey: 'We can start there. That's Gemma's class.' Abbi: 'I don't care.'
jokes.setup: After Trey offers money and oral sex, Abbi names her actual price
jokes.punchline: Her blackmail demand is just to teach a gym class — a mundane professional request that becomes funny in the context of a blackmail negotiation. Her 'I don't care' about Gemma is a new and delightful edge.
jokes.explanation: The gap between the magnitude of the secret (porn career) and the smallness of the demand (one gym class) is perfectly calibrated. Abbi's 'I don't care' about the displaced instructor shows her new Kirk-Steele-fueled confidence. Character-specific and structurally tight.

jokes.text: Trey: 'Look, can't I just go down on you?' Abbi: 'No, stop it.' Trey: 'I think it would be in your best interest—' Abbi: 'Kirk.'
jokes.setup: Trey keeps returning to oral sex as the preferred form of negotiation
jokes.punchline: Abbi shuts down the entire argument again with 'Kirk' — the name now functions as a full stop command
jokes.explanation: The 'Kirk' callback is now a running gag — the name is Pavlovian, the audience anticipates it. The comedy of 'I think it would be in your best interest' being cut off by a single word works because we've already seen it work once. The third use of this device will likely be diminishing returns, so it's smart to deploy it quickly.

jokes.text: Abbi attempts to demonstrate kettle bell form and immediately causes a disaster — 'I don't know what just happened with my body. It's like I literally have zero upper body strength.'
jokes.setup: Abbi confidently tells the class she'll show them form first
jokes.punchline: She catastrophically fails — implied to have broken a mirror — and immediately admits to zero upper body strength
jokes.explanation: Classic Abbi pattern: ambition meets physical reality. 'I literally have zero upper body strength' is a confession so complete and immediate that it's its own punchline. Good physical comedy beat even without seeing the full visual.

jokes.text: Abbi attempts to demonstrate kettle bell form, immediately drops the weight and apparently smashes a $1400 mirror.
jokes.setup: Abbi announces 'form is essential!' and begins to demonstrate
jokes.punchline: She immediately demonstrates catastrophically bad form by destroying a $1400 mirror — the gap between her confidence and her ability
jokes.explanation: A classic sitcom physical beat: the more emphatically you declare competence, the more catastrophically you fail. 'Form is essential!' as the setup for immediate catastrophic formlessness is well-telegraphed. Presumably the mirror smash is a great visual gag. The specific price ($1400) adds consequence and stakes. Works better on screen.

jokes.text: Trey: 'I have a kid.' Abbi: 'What, you do?' Trey: 'Probably! I mean, a paternity suit could pop up at any time.'
jokes.setup: Trey tries to make Abbi feel guilty for getting him in trouble, pulling out 'I have a kid'
jokes.punchline: 'Probably!' — his uncertainty about his own parenthood is both a commentary on his porn past and a beautifully specific character detail. The paternity suit framing is perfectly Trey.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'Probably!' is doing enormous work — it simultaneously reveals Trey's past (many sexual partners, porn career), deflates the emotional manipulation attempt, and creates a genuinely original character detail. 'A paternity suit could pop up at any time' has the resigned tone of someone who has made peace with this possibility. The whole exchange (sympathy bid → uncertainty → legal framing) is tightly constructed. Earns 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Trey: 'Please, Abbi, I have a kid.' Abbi: 'What, you do?' Trey: 'Probably! I mean, a paternity suit could pop up at any time.'
jokes.setup: Trey is appealing to Abbi's sympathy to take the blame for the mirror
jokes.punchline: 'Probably!' — the immediate hedge on having a child, then the explanation that a paternity suit 'could pop up at any time' — the porn star lifestyle implication played as anxious responsibility
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character-integrated comedy. The setup is a standard sympathy appeal ('I have a kid'), which Abbi believes — then Trey's correction 'Probably!' undercuts the entire premise while revealing the specific anxieties of someone with a porn past. The phrase 'a paternity suit could pop up at any time' treats potential unknown offspring as a perpetual ambient threat, like a legal weather condition. It's simultaneously absurd and psychologically specific to Trey's situation. High character integration: this joke only works for this character.

jokes.text: Ilana on the subway: 'Shh, Oliver, we don't talk about Uber down here.'
jokes.setup: Oliver mentions he usually Ubers; they're on the subway
jokes.punchline: Ilana treats Uber as a taboo secret, protecting Oliver (and herself) from class-based social judgment from other subway riders
jokes.explanation: Quick, sharp class-comedy beat. Ilana's class-consciousness expressed as a subway code of silence is both politically consistent and funny. 'We don't talk about Uber down here' has the hushed urgency of someone protecting a dangerous secret.

jokes.text: Ilana's game: find the most disadvantaged person on the subway and give them your seat — Oliver correctly identifies it's himself
jokes.setup: Ilana sets up what sounds like a consciousness-raising exercise in social equity
jokes.punchline: Oliver immediately and correctly identifies himself as the most disadvantaged — 'you are rich in money but poor in experience' — then Oliver's rebuttal: 'No, I'm poor. I get the seat.'
jokes.explanation: Oliver's 'No, I'm poor. I get the seat.' is a wonderful child-logic response that simultaneously validates his perspective and exposes Ilana's condescension. Ilana's pivot to 'you are right, that's right' and 'lesson learned' is the perfect capitulation. The comedy is in Ilana getting outmaneuvered by a child using her own framework.

jokes.text: Abbi's internal debate about raising $700: 'Sell mushroom chocolates! No, I can't do that. Can't get back in the game. Just got my record expunged.'
jokes.setup: Abbi paces trying to think of ways to raise money fast
jokes.punchline: Her first idea is drug sales — she rejects it not on moral grounds but because she literally just cleared her record. The expungement detail implies she has a history.
jokes.explanation: The expungement detail is a perfect character-depth joke — Abbi is presented as a somewhat hapless straight-man but she has a drug dealing past. 'Can't get back in the game' implies the game is always there, waiting. Dark and specific in the best Broad City way.

jokes.text: Abbi on her art: 'It's brilliant, but no one understands it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi considers selling her art as a money-raising option
jokes.punchline: She adds the caveat without any self-awareness — not 'maybe it's not accessible' but 'no one understands it' as if that's the audience's problem
jokes.explanation: Quick, economical self-delusion joke. The flat delivery implied ('brilliant, but') does a lot of work. Pays off later when we learn her art is celebrity food portraits.

jokes.text: Abbi's reason for selling clothes: 'I get compliments.' — her full justification
jokes.setup: She's considering her assets — what does she have that's worth money?
jokes.punchline: Her entire economic case for her wardrobe's resale value is 'I get compliments' — an informal endorsement treated as market data
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfect economy. The gap between compliments as social currency and compliments as financial proof is the entire joke.

jokes.text: The Beacon's Closet scene — Oliver and Abbi bonding as the buyer makes unimpressed faces at clothes Abbi is clearly emotionally attached to
jokes.setup: Abbi is selling clothes she clearly loves; the buyer is assessing them clinically
jokes.punchline: Sustained cringe as each item Abbi describes with attachment ('my mom got it for me', 'one of my favourite pieces') is rejected or minimally valued
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe comedy execution of selling beloved possessions. The visual gag of Oliver watching Abbi's emotional pain with detached interest is a nice layer. The dead-eyed buyer is the right comedic foil.

jokes.text: Abbi watching Oliver try to walk in her heels: 'But honestly, your legs look amazing. As well as my tukas?' — Oliver correcting her: 'It's tucchus, but I am loving it, brother.'
jokes.setup: Oliver tries on Abbi's shoes for sale
jokes.punchline: Oliver corrects Abbi's Yiddish pronunciation with authority, then uses 'brother' as a term of address — an eight-year-old wealthy Jewish boy channeling Ilana's energy
jokes.explanation: Oliver correcting Abbi's Yiddish is charming and funny — he clearly grew up with it, she picked it up secondhand. 'I am loving it, brother' is a child successfully performing Ilana's grown-up cool, and it's delightful.

jokes.text: Buyer: 'Oh, I saw a denim jacket like that on who wore it best? Leighton Meester wore it best.'
jokes.setup: Abbi hopefully shows a denim jacket
jokes.punchline: The buyer's unsolicited verdict that Leighton Meester wore it better is delivered as crushing small talk, not as cruelty
jokes.explanation: The specific name-drop (Leighton Meester, not a huge celebrity, giving it a petty tabloid energy) is the best part. Functional cruelty joke with decent specificity.

jokes.text: Abbi's dead aunt's apothecary bag story: 'She was a flight attendant. She died in that plane crash. It was terrible over the midwest.' — gets $20 cash offer.
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to get more money for her dead aunt's bag
jokes.punchline: The dead aunt story (which may or may not be fabricated) is deployed as a sales tactic and still only gets $20 cash; the buyer's 'so sad' is perfunctory before delivering the low offer
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'terrible over the midwest' (as opposed to, say, the ocean, which sounds more dramatic) is a nice touch — either it happened and it's funny for its mundane geography, or she's making it up and invented a midwestern plane crash. Either way the buyer's unmoved 'so sad' / $20 offer is perfectly cold.

jokes.text: The buyer's 'apothecary bag' assessment: '$226' — Abbi having sold everything including the deceased aunt's bag for $20 total, then finds the bag worth $226
jokes.setup: This is set up as a callback when they return with rich clothes
jokes.punchline: The aunt's bag that got $20 cash is priced at $226 in the store — the buyer exploited Abbi's grief and poverty at $200+ markup
jokes.explanation: This is a dark payoff to the earlier bit — the store immediately marks up the bag 10x. The callback structure rewards attentive viewers and the dark comedy of capitalist exploitation is pointed without being preachy.

jokes.text: Abbi sells her art at a street table — her description: 'Celebrity's favourite foods. Original pieces, people! Bitch's gotta buy a mirror here.'
jokes.setup: Abbi hawks her art on the street
jokes.punchline: The pitch descends from professional ('original pieces, people') to personal ('bitch's gotta buy a mirror here') — the honest motivation breaking through the sales persona
jokes.explanation: 'Bitch's gotta buy a mirror here' is good — it's the desperation of the whole situation condensed into a street-hawking slogan. The premise (celebrity food art) is also being established here as the payoff for 'it's brilliant but no one understands it.'

jokes.text: Art negotiation: Ilana offers $2 for two $40 paintings — 'I meant $2.00.' — and Abbi says 'No way' then immediately says 'okay let's do it'
jokes.setup: Ilana is 'helping' Abbi sell art and negotiates on her behalf
jokes.punchline: Ilana negotiates Abbi's price down to $2, and Abbi, after initial protest, immediately accepts — her own standards destroyed by desperation
jokes.explanation: 'It's good to have standards' / '$2.00 / okay let's do it' is a nice crumbling-dignity beat. The friendship dynamic (Ilana undermining Abbi while meaning to help) is core show material executed well.

jokes.text: Man on Ron Howard: 'Of course, shrimp cocktail's Ron Howard's favourite food. He probably has it three, four times a week, that fucking pussy.'
jokes.setup: The man is reading the celebrity food art labels
jokes.punchline: The non-sequitur hostility toward Ron Howard — he apparently has strong opinions about Ron Howard's character based on his presumed shrimp cocktail habit
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the insult — shrimp cocktail as evidence of a particular kind of effete cowardice — is genuinely funny and weird. 'Three, four times a week' as the damning frequency makes the hostility more precise. The character (an angry man with strong feelings about celebrity diets) is a good one-scene bit player.

jokes.text: The celebrity food art itself is revealed: Ron Howard's favourite food is shrimp cocktail; Bruce Springsteen's is a tomato because he's from Jersey
jokes.setup: A customer examines Abbi's 'celebrity favourite foods' artwork
jokes.punchline: The art is revealed as completely made up — but Abbi's New Jersey logic for Springsteen/tomato ('that's a marriage, you know, that works') is presented with genuine artistic conviction
jokes.explanation: The payoff to 'it's brilliant but no one understands it' is perfect — of course no one understands it, because it's entirely fictional. But Abbi's artistic justification ('he's from Jersey, so I put him with a tomato, 'cause I was like, that's a marriage') is so earnest and so wrong-headed that it works as both a character joke and a parody of artistic pretension. Ron Howard/shrimp cocktail has an inexplicable confidence that makes it funnier than if it were plausible. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Angry customer: 'Is it that unbelievable? Forty doll... I give $40, I'm gonna get a little more than artwork from you over there, sweet cheeks.'
jokes.setup: Customer is outraged to learn the celebrity food art is fictional
jokes.punchline: His entitled suggestion that $40 entitles him to more than art ('a little more than artwork') — making a clumsy pass while ostensibly complaining about fraud
jokes.explanation: The customer's outrage-to-come-on pivot is a decent beat but the character isn't developed enough to land huge. 'Sweet cheeks' is the right level of gross. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Why don't you fuck my fucking dick?' — aggressive non-sequitur response to the customer's pass
jokes.setup: Customer makes an entitled pass at Abbi
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck my fucking dick' is anatomically impossible slang used as an aggressive dismissal — Ilana's influence on Abbi's language is on full display
jokes.explanation: The anatomical confusion is funny — 'my fucking dick' from Abbi, who doesn't have one, channeling street aggression she's clearly borrowed from Ilana. It's a character-growth joke: Abbi is becoming more assertive, but still slightly off in her execution.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Hey, listen, I've been there, sweetie. I've been there.' Customer: 'To my dick?' Ilana: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Ilana steps in to mediate after Abbi's 'fuck my dick' comment
jokes.punchline: The customer's confused 'to my dick?' and Ilana's flat 'yeah' — she confirms sexual history with a stranger as casually as confirming she's been to Brooklyn
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The compression is extraordinary: Ilana's 'I've been there' is meant as consolation, the customer misreads it, and Ilana's 'yeah' confirms the misreading without batting an eye. Three beats, perfect economy, completely character-defining. Only Ilana would confirm casual sex history with a stranger as a de-escalation tactic, and be completely unbothered by it. Earns 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi's total: '$22 still.' — their running fundraising total is $22 after all that effort
jokes.setup: After all the clothes selling, art selling, negotiating, and chaos
jokes.punchline: Twenty-two dollars. The gap between the effort expended and the result is a joke in itself.
jokes.explanation: Good structural beat — the ongoing tally ($22) sets up the contrast with the windfall about to come. Functional but not a great standalone joke.

jokes.text: Oliver to Abbi's employer: 'We hung out with Abbi, Ilana's girlfriend, even though they can't be together right now. But they will be someday.'
jokes.setup: Oliver is recounting his day to his mother
jokes.punchline: He frames Abbi and Ilana's friendship as a romantic relationship with an uncertain future — the certainty of 'they will be someday' is played as child wisdom about their obvious connection
jokes.explanation: Oliver's sincere analysis of the Abbi-Ilana relationship as a delayed romance is both funny (a child as relationship analyst) and touching. 'They will be someday' has the confidence of a child who sees something the adults won't admit. Good meta-commentary on the show's central relationship.

jokes.text: Employer: 'I had no idea you were gay.' Ilana: 'We all...' [trails off, doesn't complete the sentence]
jokes.setup: Oliver outs Ilana as gay based on his reading of her relationship with Abbi
jokes.punchline: Ilana begins to respond — 'we all...' — and simply stops, as if the sentence would be too large to complete. A perfect suspension in the middle of ambiguity.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy through incompletion. 'We all...' stops because the full statement — 'we all have a little gay in us,' or 'we all love Abbi,' or something else entirely — would collapse into a mess. Ilana's trailing off IS the joke: she can't deny, won't confirm, and the unfinished thought contains her entire sexuality and her feelings for Abbi simultaneously. Purely character-specific, impossible to imagine any other character using this non-answer. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Employer decides having a 'gayby-sitter' will be wonderful for Oliver's CV to get into middle school
jokes.setup: Employer just learned Ilana is 'gay'
jokes.punchline: She immediately reduces Ilana's sexuality to a credential useful for Oliver's middle school applications — treating gayness as a diversity checkbox on a child's CV
jokes.explanation: 'Gayby-sitter' is a good portmanteau, and the class satire (sexuality as CV material for middle school) is pointed. The employer's complete unselfconsciousness about the tokenism is the joke.

jokes.text: Employer deliberates about giving clothes to homeless people vs. horses: 'The horses do get very cold in the winter, but on the other, the less fortunate probably get just as cold, maybe even more so.'
jokes.setup: Helen is asked to donate last season's clothes to the homeless via Ilana
jokes.punchline: She genuinely weighs humans vs. horses for charity, giving horses a real chance at winning the debate before conceding humans 'probably' get just as cold — with 'maybe even more so' hedging as a concession
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly calibrated class satire. The horror is in her total sincerity — she's not joking, she genuinely considered the horses first. 'Maybe even more so' as her ultimate acknowledgment of human suffering (hedged!) is perfectly written. The joke works because the character fully commits to the position before being walked back by her own reasoning. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Ilana's complaint then immediate plan: 'Come on, dude, I thought we were cool. Now I gotta lug all this shit to some Jesus house?' — followed immediately by Oliver's scheme to resell at Beacon's Closet
jokes.setup: Ilana is burdened with hauling fancy rich-lady clothes to charity
jokes.punchline: Oliver immediately identifies the arbitrage opportunity — sell the fancy clothes, keep the cash. 'Jesus house' as her term for a charity is a good Ilana-ism.
jokes.explanation: 'Jesus house' is the best detail here — a completely non-judgmental but oddly specific term for a charity. Oliver's entrepreneurial instinct is a nice reversal of Ilana's earlier education of him.

jokes.text: 'The student has become the teacher.' — Ilana's reaction to Oliver's Beacon's Closet plan
jokes.setup: Oliver just taught Ilana a hustle she didn't think of
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers the cliché completely straight and sincerely — it's a genuine moment of pedagogical pride
jokes.explanation: The cliché is the joke here — Ilana using it completely earnestly when the 'student' outsmarted her in a hustling scheme she should have known. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oliver whispering 'Yes, queen!' while Ilana mouths it silently — a child absorbing Ilana's vernacular
jokes.setup: Ilana has been saying 'yes, queen' throughout; Oliver has absorbed it
jokes.punchline: Oliver has learned 'yes, queen' from Ilana and deploys it with total sincerity; Ilana mouths it silently in parallel, making it a shared moment of celebration
jokes.explanation: Oliver saying 'yes, queen' is a genuine payoff of the Ilana-is-molding-Oliver thread. The visual of them synced up on the phrase is a sweet/funny character beat.

jokes.text: Oliver asking 'Do the homeless like Prada?' — completely sincere
jokes.setup: They're about to go sell fancy Prada clothes under the guise of donating
jokes.punchline: Oliver's question is entirely earnest — he has no framework for what the homeless would want in clothing beyond brand prestige
jokes.explanation: Oliver asking about Prada preferences of homeless people is a perfect encapsulation of his class bubble. The question is so genuinely innocent that it's both funny and a little sad. Good character economy.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana return to Beacon's Closet with designer clothes; the worker at first doesn't recognize them ('Remember me?') and the Pretty Woman reference begins
jokes.setup: Just hours after their humiliating experience selling cheap clothes for $20
jokes.punchline: They've returned with Prada bags and designer items; the employee still doesn't remember them — 'Even less.'
jokes.explanation: 'Even less' is a good deadpan beat — the employee's memory of them has somehow gotten worse despite another interaction. Sets up the Pretty Woman homage sequence effectively.

jokes.text: The Pretty Woman callback: Abbi and Ilana return to Beacon's with better clothes — 'Remember me?' / 'Big mistake. Big. Huge.'
jokes.setup: Having been dismissed and received only $22 from their first trip, they return with Helen's fancy wardrobe
jokes.punchline: They explicitly reenact the Pretty Woman shopping scene — 'Big mistake. Big. Huge.' — with total commitment
jokes.explanation: The Pretty Woman reference is unambiguous and the situation earns it structurally — they did get dismissed, they did come back with money. The execution is committed enough to work even as a well-worn reference. 'Even less' (the buyer recognizing them even less than before) is a nice inversion.

jokes.text: 'Big mistake. Big. Huge.' [Pretty Woman reference]
jokes.setup: The Pretty Woman triumphant return structure has been set up
jokes.punchline: The verbatim quote — but it lands because the situation (hipster resale shop) vs. the reference (Rodeo Drive boutique) creates a comic scale mismatch
jokes.explanation: The pure quotation scores lower because it's the reference doing the work, not the writing. The pop culture deployment isn't twisted enough — they just do the bit. The 'even less' subversion is funnier than this. Scored down for relying entirely on borrowed material without a transformative spin.

jokes.text: Abbi celebrates '$13,000 in store credit' — Ilana immediately: 'Dude, I need the money.'
jokes.setup: The Beacon's buyer offers $13,000 store credit OR $903 cash
jokes.punchline: Abbi celebrates the store credit as if it's a win; Ilana has to remind her they need actual cash for an actual mirror
jokes.explanation: Quick beat on Abbi's tendency to get distracted by shiny numbers. The $13,000 vs. $903 cash gap is its own commentary on store credit scams. Functional.

jokes.text: Abbi asks what her dead aunt's apothecary bag is now priced at in the store: '$226.' Beat. 'Wonderful.'
jokes.setup: The aunt's bag was bought for $20 cash
jokes.punchline: 'Wonderful.' — Abbi's single-word response to learning the 10x markup contains all her grief, frustration, and powerlessness in one word delivered with deadly calm
jokes.explanation: The payoff is exceptional. '$226.' / beat / 'Wonderful.' — that single word in that context is doing enormous comedic work: it's sarcastic, it's defeated, it's dignified, and it contains Abbi's entire relationship to economic powerlessness. The structure (setup 30 minutes earlier, callback at precisely the right moment, single-word response) is professional-grade comedy writing. Earns 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Abbi's parting shot: 'By the way... I know that shirt's from Forever 21. You're not kidding anybody.'
jokes.setup: After getting exploited on the apothecary bag
jokes.punchline: Abbi fires back by clocking the buyer's own fast-fashion hypocrisy — she can see through the buyer's curated vintage aesthetic
jokes.explanation: A satisfying small victory for Abbi after the $226 exploitation. The Forever 21 callout is the kind of petty, accurate revenge that's completely Abbi — not big, not confrontational, just precisely targeted. 'Nice.' is the buyer's minimal acknowledgment, which is its own funny beat.

jokes.text: The mirror installation scene — Abbi and Trey wrestling with the mirror in the dark gym after hours
jokes.setup: They need to secretly reinstall a $1400 mirror at night
jokes.punchline: The physical comedy of two people trying to hang a large mirror badly — 'ease your grip... looks good... they're not gonna know, right? No one will ever notice.'
jokes.explanation: Functional visual comedy beat. 'They're not gonna know, right?' as they clearly do a terrible job is a mild gag. The scene's comedy is more in the situation than the dialogue.

jokes.text: Abbi and Trey installing the mirror after hours. Trey: 'Looks good.' Abbi: 'Yeah. They're not gonna know, right?' Trey: 'No one will ever notice.' [Cut to the clearly crooked mirror]
jokes.setup: They've installed the replacement mirror at night to hide their responsibility
jokes.punchline: Their mutual reassurance that it's unnoticeable is immediately contradicted by the visual of the obviously crooked/wrong installation
jokes.explanation: A reliable visual gag type — mutual self-delusion about something objectively visible. Works on screen, less in transcript. The structure is classic but not novel.

jokes.text: Trey ambushes a nearly-asleep Abbi: 'Not so fast, muchacha. I need you right here.' — there's no pube situation, but she has to teach a class
jokes.setup: Abbi says she's going home to sleep forever until her 10:30 shift
jokes.punchline: 'Not so fast, muchacha' — Trey's random Spanish endearment as he reveals another demand; the pube fake-out (she knows there can't be any) sets up the real reveal
jokes.explanation: 'Muchacha' as a Trey-ism is a nice character detail. The pube beat ('Trey, there's no way there's a pube situation. No one else is here. Do you put your pubes somewhere?') is a funny callback to the running pube thread.

jokes.text: 'This is a bazinga. I swear, I've lost all respect for you, Trey.' — Abbi uses 'bazinga' to call out a fake-out
jokes.setup: Trey hints at needing her to clean up pubes again
jokes.punchline: Abbi deploys 'bazinga' — the Big Bang Theory catchphrase — as her word for a fake-out, then says she's 'lost all respect for you, Trey' when he confirms it's not a bazinga
jokes.explanation: The joke has layers: Abbi uses 'bazinga' (already slightly dated/cringe) as a synonym for a prank, then expresses more disappointment when it's real than when it was fake. 'I've lost all respect for you, Trey' being about the bazinga rather than about the actual exploitation she's endured is character-specific irony.

jokes.text: Trey's flattery: 'Abbi, you're such a good cleaner. You're so special.' — as his sincere compliment to get her to clean
jokes.setup: Trey needs Abbi to clean before the class
jokes.punchline: His entire manipulation toolkit is 'you're a good cleaner, you're special' — complimenting her on the most demeaning aspect of her job as if it's a gift
jokes.explanation: Trey using 'good cleaner' as a sincere compliment reveals his entire view of Abbi and the power dynamic. The delivery of 'you're so special' as a manipulation is funny because it's so transparent and so specific.

jokes.text: Regina has been missing for 48 hours — Trey: 'Plus Regina who normally teaches the class has been missing for 48 hours.' Ilana: 'Love Regina, but that's awesome.'
jokes.setup: Trey explains why Abbi can teach the class
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response to a woman being missing for 48 hours is 'Love Regina, but that's awesome' — because it means Abbi gets her chance. Total moral callousness in service of a friend.
jokes.explanation: 'Love Regina, but that's awesome' is perfectly Ilana. The 'Love Regina' disclaimer is her only acknowledgment of ethics before completely setting it aside. The brevity and matter-of-factness of ignoring a missing person report in favor of Abbi's career opportunity is both dark and laugh-out-loud funny. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi's class speech: 'What's up, guys? Are you ready to rise to your potential?' — the class responds with enthusiastic 'Yeah! Let's do this!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is finally teaching her dream class
jokes.punchline: The irony is that she has no idea what she's doing — the enthusiastic crowd responding to her generic inspirational question sets up the imminent disaster
jokes.explanation: Setup beat for the physical comedy sequence to follow. The enthusiasm of the class is the contrast element.

jokes.text: The Kirk Steele workout class: Abbi channels Kirk Steele's porn persona, introducing herself as 'I'm Kirk Steele. I'm 18, I'm from Orlando, Florida and my favorite movie is Garden State. Are you ready to explore the infinite abyss?'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been watching Trey's (Kirk Steele's) old porn; she now channels his character to teach the class
jokes.punchline: Kirk Steele is an 18-year-old from Orlando whose favorite movie is Garden State — the specific, perfectly dated choices make this a portrait of early-2000s corn-fed mid-range porn. 'Are you ready to explore the infinite abyss?' as a workout prompt is sublime.
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak. Every detail is perfectly calibrated to evoke the early-2000s porn aesthetic: 18 years old (barely legal trope subverted earlier), Orlando (maximum provincial innocence), Garden State (peak 2004 indie earnestness), 'explore the infinite abyss' (Garden State's actual dialogue repurposed as workout instruction). This is layered comedy — it's funny as a porn parody, funny as a Garden State parody, funny as a workout class, and funny as character work (Abbi losing herself in the role). The specificity is extraordinary. Earns 9+ on craft.

jokes.text: Mid-workout, Abbi as Kirk Steele: 'When I grow up I either want to be a stunt choreographer or I want to work with kids with MS.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is in full Kirk Steele mode during the workout
jokes.punchline: The career aspirations of an 18-year-old porn actor — delivered mid-exercise — include 'work with kids with MS' as a humanitarian goal equal to stunt choreography
jokes.explanation: The addition of 'work with kids with MS' to stunt choreography captures the porn-actor-with-a-heart-of-gold cliché. The juxtaposition is specific (MS in particular, not a generic charity) and the phrasing is exactly how an 18-year-old would express aspirational humanitarianism. Delivered mid-exercise adds to the absurdity. Earns 8+.
